analysis: The stronger solutions share a common architecture: (a) prune dominated candidates to reduce N, (b) approximate hypervolume with a shared Monte‑Carlo sample set and precomputed domination masks (built in memory‑safe blocks), (c) perform a lazy greedy selection using a max‑heap of estimated marginal gains and an incremental covered mask, and (d) polish only a tiny shortlist with exact HV (pygmo) or mask‑based swaps. Key implementation optimizations: use index lists when rows are sparse, vectorized boolean ops (masks & ~covered) and count_nonzero for true gains, early stop when samples are fully covered, and scale sample M with k and problem dimension. Weak designs recompute exact HV per candidate in deep nested loops (heavy pg.hypervolume calls), do many redundant HV object constructions, or run large evolutionary populations without tight bounds, causing high overhead and unstable k‑cardinality handling. Good fallbacks (box‑volume, distance seeding, farthest‑first) and deterministic RNG seeding appear across better heuristics to make runtime predictable and fill slots when sampling misses contribution.
experience: Prune dominated points, precompute shared MC samples and boolean masks in memory‑safe blocks, then run a lazy greedy heap with incremental covered masks. Use sparse index lists when appropriate, keep sample size proportional to k and D, and restrict exact HV calls to a tiny shortlisted pool for bounded swap polishing.
current_reflection: Prune dominated points, precompute shared MC samples and boolean masks in memory‑safe blocks, then run a lazy greedy heap with incremental covered masks. Use sparse index lists when appropriate, keep sample size proportional to k and D, and restrict exact HV calls to a tiny shortlisted pool for bounded swap polishing.
bad_reflections: Prune dominated/ref‑worse points, use one shared MC sample set computed in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean masks, run lazy greedy via a max‑heap with incremental overlap updates, tie‑break by cheap box‑volumes, and do a bounded exact polish on a small shortlist. Cap samples/iterations, vectorize inner work, reuse hypervolume objects, and seed RNGs for reproducibility.

Prune dominated points, build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, use a lazy greedy heap with incremental overlap updates, then polish only a small shortlist with exact HV. Cache hypervolume objects, scale sample count with k and D, and cap refinement iterations. These give best quality per runtime.

Always prune dominated points, reuse one MC sample set and precomputed masks (use sparse lists when sparse), perform lazy greedy with incremental overlap updates, and limit exact HV calls to a small shortlisted pool with capped iterations. Tune sample count adaptively with k and D and block boolean work to be memory‑safe. Avoid recomputing hypervolume objects inside inner loops.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: prune dominated, shared MC samples, memory-safe blocks, boolean/sparse masks, lazy-greedy heap, incremental masks, sample~k·D, shortlist, exact HV, bounded polish
- Advice: Prune dominated/ref‑worse points; build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks; precompute boolean or sparse masks; run lazy greedy with a max‑heap and incremental covered‑mask updates; tie‑break by cheap box volumes; scale samples ~k·D; limit exact hypervolume calls to a tiny shortlisted pool, cache HV objects, seed RNGs.
- Avoid: Recomputing samples/masks or hypervolume objects inside inner loops; large monolithic mask arrays that exceed memory; unbounded exact HV polishing.
- Explanation: Reuse and block heavy work to save time and memory, scale sampling with k and D, and bound expensive exact HV calls for high quality per runtime.
